member_b_label: Fusarium Wilts, Blights, Rots And Damping-Off

graph.confusable_reason: Both are soilborne fungal diseases of melon that cause vascular/root crown infection leading to wilting, vine decline, and plant collapse, often mid-to-late season. Both produce internal vascular discoloration and can kill plants outright, so foliar wilt symptoms alone are ambiguous. They are separable by the diagnostic SIGN inside affected tissue: Macrophomina produces tiny black microsclerotia peppering the crown/root, while Fusarium produces salmon-pink/white mycelium and conidia and classic one-sided vascular browning.
graph.shared_presentation: Mid- to late-season melon plants showing progressive wilting, yellowing/decline of vines, and eventual collapse; cutting the lower stem or crown reveals internal vascular/cortical discoloration, and affected roots/crowns show rot. Both favor stressed plants and warm soils, and both can be mistaken for drought or other vine-decline complexes.

forks.question: What soil temperature and moisture regime preceded symptom onset?
forks.a_signal: Hot (28-35C), dry soils and drought/heat-stressed plants; symptoms flare under high-temperature water stress
forks.b_signal: Warm but more moderate soils; symptoms appear across a wider moisture range and can worsen with adequate-to-high moisture

forks.question: How quickly do plants decline and is wilt one-sided?
forks.a_signal: Often rapid collapse of whole plant during heat, frequently near fruit load; wilt typically affects the whole plant
forks.b_signal: Slower, progressive wilt that often begins one-sided (single runners/leaves yellow first) before whole-plant decline

forks.question: Where is the primary lesion concentrated?
forks.a_signal: Crown and lower stem/taproot at/below soil line, with dry shredded cortex
forks.b_signal: Lower stem/crown with vascular streaking extending up the stem and into runners

forks.question: What does the cut vascular/cortical tissue look like in color and texture?
forks.a_signal: Grayish-black to silvery dry rot of the cortex and stele; tissue ashy and brittle
forks.b_signal: Tan to reddish-brown vascular browning, often a continuous streak up one side; tissue not ashy

forks.question: Split the crown/lower stem and examine the tissue and rot surface with a hand lens for the pathogen sign.
forks.a_signal: Abundant tiny (pinpoint, ~0.1mm) jet-black microsclerotia peppered through and on the gray tissue, giving a 'charcoal'/sooty speckled appearance
forks.b_signal: No black specks; salmon-pink to white fungal mycelium/sporodochia on the surface under humidity, with brown vascular streaking

graph.narrative_a: Charcoal rot (Macrophomina phaseolina) on melon is a hot-weather, stress-driven disease. It infects the crown, lower stem, and taproot, producing a dry gray-to-silvery cortical rot. Plants often collapse suddenly during periods of high temperature and drought, frequently as fruit load increases. The diagnostic feature is the dense pepper-like dusting of minute jet-black microsclerotia embedded in and on the ashy tissue, visible with a hand lens or even the naked eye, which gives the disease its 'charcoal' name. Diagnosis is confirmed by splitting the crown/root and observing this sooty black speckling against gray, brittle tissue.
graph.narrative_b: Fusarium (Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. melonis and related species) causes wilt, crown/root rot, and damping-off in melon. Wilt commonly begins one-sided, with individual runners or leaves yellowing and wilting before the whole plant declines, and is associated with tan-to-reddish-brown vascular streaking visible when the stem is split lengthwise. Under humid conditions, salmon-pink to white mycelium and spore masses (sporodochia) may form on infected stem surfaces. There are NO black microsclerotia. Fusarium can also kill seedlings (damping-off) and is strongly governed by host race and resistance genetics. Confirmation rests on the brown vascular streak plus pink/white fungal signs and absence of charcoal speckling.
graph.management_a: Manage water/heat stress through adequate irrigation scheduling to avoid drought during fruit fill; avoid plant stress and over-fruiting. Rotate away from other susceptible hosts (Macrophomina is broad-host), incorporate residue to reduce inoculum, and maintain balanced fertility. No strong genetic resistance is available, so cultural stress reduction and soil moisture management are primary; soil solarization can reduce microsclerotia in some regions.
graph.management_b: Use Fusarium-resistant melon cultivars matched to the local pathogen race (primary tool). Rotate out of cucurbits for several years, use clean/treated seed and pathogen-free transplants, plant into well-drained warm soils, and avoid moving infested soil. Grafting onto resistant rootstocks and soil fumigation/solarization help in high-pressure fields. Manage seedbeds to prevent damping-off with sanitation and seed treatment.

lay.farmer_lay_report: My melon vines are wilting and going downhill in the heat of mid- to late-season, with leaves yellowing and the plants slowly collapsing. When I slice open the lower stem or crown, the inside is discolored and streaky, and the roots and crown look rotted.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Look at the gray, dried-out inner stem tissue for a fine peppering of tiny jet-black specks that look like someone dusted it with sooty charcoal or ground pepper. The black flecks are pinpoint-sized, scattered through and over the grayish tissue.
lay.decisive_lay_b: No black sooty specks. Instead, after sitting in a humid spot (like a closed bag), the rotted stem or crown grows a salmon-pink to creamy-white fuzzy or moldy film on the surface, and the streaks running up inside the stem are brown.
